questions.question: Which is an RFC 1918 private network?
questions.explanation: 192.168.0.0/16 is one of the three RFC 1918 ranges.

correct.explanation: 192.168.0.0/16 is one of the three RFC 1918 ranges.

incorrect.explanation: "8.8.8.0/24" is incorrect because the scenario requires: 192.168.0.0/16 is one of the three RFC 1918 ranges.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "203.0.113.0/24" is incorrect because the scenario requires: 192.168.0.0/16 is one of the three RFC 1918 ranges.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Picking a familiar term without matching the exact behavior tested

incorrect.explanation: "169.254.1.0/24" is incorrect because the scenario requires: 192.168.0.0/16 is one of the three RFC 1918 ranges.

questions.question: A Windows PC shows IP 169.254.88.3. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: 169.254.x.x is APIPA — self-assigned when DHCP fails.

correct.explanation: 169.254.x.x is APIPA — self-assigned when DHCP fails.

questions.question: Why must private addresses use NAT to reach the Internet?
choices: They are not globally unique and are not routed publicly
questions.explanation: Private space is reused and non-routable on the Internet — NAT maps to a public address.

correct.explanation: Private space is reused and non-routable on the Internet — NAT maps to a public address.

questions.question: True or False: APIPA addresses are in the same RFC 1918 private ranges as 192.168.x.x.
questions.explanation: False — APIPA uses 169.254.0.0/16, separate from RFC 1918.

correct.explanation: False — APIPA uses 169.254.0.0/16, separate from RFC 1918.

incorrect.explanation: "True" is incorrect because the scenario requires: False — APIPA uses 169.254.0.0/16, separate from RFC 1918.

questions.question: Many PCs on 192.168.1.0/24 share one ISP public IP. Which technology enables this?
questions.explanation: NAT (often PAT) translates many private addresses to one or few public IPs.

correct.explanation: NAT (often PAT) translates many private addresses to one or few public IPs.

incorrect.explanation: "STP" is incorrect because the scenario requires: NAT (often PAT) translates many private addresses to one or few public IPs.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Confusing inside local, inside global, and outside addresses

incorrect.explanation: "VTP" is incorrect because the scenario requires: NAT (often PAT) translates many private addresses to one or few public IPs.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Confusing inside local, inside global, and outside addresses

incorrect.explanation: "LLDP" is incorrect because the scenario requires: NAT (often PAT) translates many private addresses to one or few public IPs.
incorrect.misconceptionTested: Confusing inside local, inside global, and outside addresses

answerReview.examTip: Mark inside/outside interfaces first — NAT does nothing without them.
